texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study investigates a Direct Air Capture (DAC) process using a monoethanolamine (MEA) absorption process, further enhanced by the integration of heat pump modifications such as Lean Vapor Compression (LVC) and Stripper Overhead Compression (SOC) in the regeneration stage to reduce reboiler requirement. The process is modelled using Aspen Plus and incorporating the CCSI model adapted and suited for DAC environmental conditions. Technical performance was investigated through a sensitivity analysis of various environmental and process parameters, including MEA emission, air inlet temperature and humidity, and factors such as solvent loading, capture rate, gas velocity, and liquid to gas ratio. A scrubber section above the absorber was installed to mitigate MEA emission. The implementation of LVC and SOC results in a significant reduction in regeneration consumption of 25% compared to conventional MEA-based processes but, because of additional capital investment, the cost of capture is similar to that of the conventional process. The regulatory limits on MEA emission at the air outlet have a significant impact on the dimensions of the scrubber section and therefore the overall cost of capture is reduced by 8 % for the 6 ppm-mol emission limit in comparison to the 0.1 ppm-mol limit. Detailed techno-economic analyses were performed for design cases with capture scales ranging from 0.1 MtCO

The general principle of SOC is that the gas stream leaving the top of the stripper is compressed without prior water condensation. The high-pressure gas stream is partially condensed at high pressure and the heat released by condensation is used to provide heat for the reboiler. The condensate is separated in a flash vessel. A new SOC stage can be added downstream if the techno-economic analysis is favorable. This study investigates the number of SOC stages in the range of 1-3. Finally, the gaseous stream is cooled to 30°C, purified and compressed to 150 bars in the CPU. This configuration reduces the reboiler duty by utilizing the heat released during condensation and reduces the energy required by the compressors in the CPU. As mentioned earlier, the potential for these modifications may be limited, specifically the maximum acceptable temperature at the inlet and outlet of the compressors.
texts.text: , is compressed to the stripper pressure, and then combined with a portion of the 30°C Reflux (from the condenser) to limit the temperature to 121°C and is finally injected at the bottom of the stripper. The general principle of SOC is that the gas stream leaving the top of the stripper is compressed without prior water condensation. The high-pressure gas stream is partially condensed at high pressure and the heat released by condensation is used to provide heat for the reboiler. The condensate is separated in a flash vessel. A new SOC stage can be added downstream if the techno-economic analysis is favorable. This study investigates the number of SOC stages in the range of 1-3. Finally, the gaseous stream is cooled to 30°C, purified and compressed to 150 bars in the CPU. This configuration reduces the reboiler duty by utilizing the heat released during condensation and reduces the energy required by the compressors in the CPU. As mentioned earlier, the potential for these modifications may be limited, specifically the maximum acceptable temperature at the inlet and outlet of the compressors.

texts.orig: O and MEA losses, pressure drop, and fan and pump work, are almost the same for each case. However, the LVC and SOC cases result in a significant reduction of the reboiler duty compared to the Conv-case. The LVC-case offers a reboiler duty gain of 29%, while the SOC1-case, SOC2-case and SOC3-case offer even higher gains of 10%, 57% and 72%, respectively. However, the energy gain is balanced by a CAPEX penalty due to LVC and SOC modifications in the regeneration process. The electric work of the compressors in the SOC or LVC modifications partially offsets the reboiler duty, making the SOC and LVC cases more electricity-intensive than the conventional process. Additionally, compressing the vapor at the top of the stripper in the SOC modification reduces the compression need of the CPU, with electricity reduction in CPU of 13%, 22% and 33%, respectively, for SOC1-case, SOC2-case and SOC3-case.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Based on economic evaluations, the SOC3-case is the most cost-effective process, followed by the SOC2-case and LVC-case. Only the SOC1-case is more expensive than the Conv-case. However, when considering industrial applications, adding architectural modifications such as LVC or SOC can increase the likelihood of risks (e.g., reliability, availability, maintainability, and safety) and contingencies. Furthermore, the conventional configuration is well-established in flue gas capture, while the LVC configuration has still little industrial feedback and the SOC configuration has a low Technology Readiness Level (TRL).
